# Feedback, Evidence, and Decision {#sec-feedback-verification-trust}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"Program testing can be used to show the presence of bugs, but never to show their absence!"*
>
> — Edsger W. Dijkstra, *Notes on Structured Programming* (1970)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Edsger W. Dijkstra, a Turing Award-winning pioneer in algorithm design, highlighted the fundamental limitation of testing. Here the quote frames rejection checks. Tests do not prove an architecture claim complete; they can identify specific failures and block unsupported candidates before a transition is authorized.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 does not ask architects to trust an AI-assisted system. It asks them to record the feedback supporting a claim, judge whether that feedback is adequate evidence, and identify who is authorized to make the resulting decision. The design-loop card indexes the claim, supporting evidence, contrary evidence and failed checks, the resulting rejection, revision, escalation, or waiver, and the decision with its owner. Confidence, fluent explanation, and a stronger tool result do not authorize a transition on their own.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What evidence supports an AI-assisted claim, what counterevidence can block it,
and who may authorize the proposed transition?
:::

The method roles in @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization operate
inside a design loop. This chapter asks what evidence can support their output
and who may authorize the next transition. The card records the claim,
available feedback and evidence, failed checks, rejected alternatives, review
result, and decision owner. Before accepting a recommendation, reviewers should
know what was measured, which assumptions limit the result, what evidence
contradicts it, which declared check can block it, and who may authorize it.

Generated output and verified evidence are different. A model can generate a plausible
architecture description. A search method can find a strong proxy score. A
surrogate model can rank candidates. A tool-using agent[^fn-ml-tool-using-agent] can call simulators and
summarize results. None of those actions authorizes a transition by itself.
Review begins with a record of what was measured, why it was relevant, how much
it cost, what assumptions it used, where the feedback is weak, which
alternatives failed, which declared check can block the result, and who owns any
waiver or final decision.

[^fn-ml-tool-using-agent]: A tool-using agent is an AI system that can programmatically invoke external software tools or APIs, such as simulators or synthesis flows, to carry out a task. In this chapter its output counts as feedback rather than authorization. The study must still record provenance and subject the result to a declared check whose failure can block the proposed transition.

Architecture 2.0 therefore needs decision rules as clearly specified as its
representations, environments, and methods. At their center is
verification, used here more broadly than its usual formal-methods sense.

> **Verification.** Verification is used broadly in this chapter. It includes
> formal methods when formal properties are available, but it also includes type
> checks, interface checks, regression tests, baseline replay, simulator
> cross-checks, synthesis constraints, physical-design warnings, security
> review, workload coverage, and expert design review. The common requirement is
> rejection capability. The check must be able to fail visibly rather than
> merely restate the method's output. As a transition becomes harder to reverse,
> the loop also needs stronger independence from the method that produced the candidate.

Verification includes any declared check whose failure can stop an invalid or
unsupported candidate, not a single tool category
(@tbl-verification-authorities).
Depending on the task, that may be a parser, test suite, simulator comparison,
synthesis constraint, deployment monitor, or expert review.

The familiar examples are prosaic for a reason. A parser rejects malformed IR,
a protocol checker rejects an illegal transaction, and a regression test blocks
a change that breaks expected behavior. Each is valuable because it can stop
the artifact from moving to the next state, not because it comments on quality.

The table groups checks by the failure class they can detect; it does not rank
them. A syntax check may be decisive for one transition while a workload,
security, or expert-review check is decisive for another. Each row names the
representative checks and the transition a failure can block, so a claim can
identify a specific rejection path rather than make a generic promise of quality
control.

| **Verification scope** | **Representative checks or signals** | **What a failure can block** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Syntax and interface checks | Parsers, type checks, API checks, ISA or ABI conformance, and schema validation. | Artifacts that cannot legally enter the loop. |
| Functional checks | Unit tests, reference outputs, assertions, formal equivalence checking, and regression suites. | Candidates that compute the wrong behavior before performance matters. |
| Model and workload checks | Baseline replay, sensitivity studies, calibration, coverage, and drift tests. | Proxy wins outside the represented workload or calibrated support. |
| Implementation checks | Synthesis constraints, timing closure, power envelopes, area budgets, gate-level simulation (GLS), and design-rule checks (DRC). | Architecture candidates that violate physical limits or fail pre-silicon validation. |
| Operational checks | SLOs, canary rollouts, rollback, telemetry, reliability, security, and incident review. | Claims that do not survive deployment conditions or policy boundaries. |
| Expert review | Design review, waived-warning review, evidence-adequacy review, and residual-risk review. | Results whose evidence is too weak for the proposed transition. |

: **Checks that can reject a claim.** A credible claim should state
which check can detect a failure in syntax, function, model support,
implementation feasibility, or deployment behavior, how independent it is from
the producer, what transition its failure blocks, and who holds the
corresponding rejection authority. {#tbl-verification-authorities tbl-colwidths="[21,36,33]"}

For each check, the study should state what it examines, which failure it
detects, and what action its failure stops under the declared policy.

@tbl-trust-record-map follows a claim from its supporting and contradictory
evidence through review and final decision. Available time and tools limit
how much evidence the study can collect. The design-loop card provides an index,
while detailed cost, evidence, check, waiver, and decision records support it.
Proxy mismatch and security boundaries test whether the chain remains credible
when metrics are gamed or evidence cannot be disclosed publicly.

| **Chain stage** | **Visible record** | **Reviewer question** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Claim** | Bounded task, workload, objectives, constraints, baseline, and proposed transition. | What exactly is being asserted, and how far is it asking to go? |
| **Feedback and cost** | Available checks, observations, latency, cost, coverage, and fidelity. | What could the loop observe within its resources? |
| **Supporting evidence** | Claim-relevant observations with provenance, assumptions, uncertainty, and scope. | Which observations actually support the claim? |
| **Counterevidence and failed checks** | Failed runs, rejected alternatives, disagreements, missing coverage, and unresolved warnings. | What weakens or blocks the claim? |
| **Review result** | Passed and failed conditions, rejection, revision, escalation, and any waiver. | Why did the claim stop, change, or continue? |
| **Authorized decision** | Commit-right holder, consequences, accepted residual risk, and reopening conditions. | Who may authorize this transition, and what are they accepting? |

: **Evidence supports only the action under review.** Completing the card does not establish that the evidence is adequate, and adequate evidence does not replace approval from the person responsible for the decision.
{#tbl-trust-record-map tbl-colwidths="[22,37,31]"}

The design-loop card is the summary and index for this chain. Detailed
observations remain in supporting records, and a replay package bundles the
card, inputs, runs, checks, and decision records when the study claims
replayability. Card structure, evidence adequacy, and authorized decision scope
remain separate questions.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can review the decision chain for an AI-assisted loop
output.
That means you can:

- **Judge** whether feedback is relevant evidence by examining fidelity, provenance, uncertainty, and claim scope;
- **Trace** a claim through supporting evidence, contrary evidence, failed checks, review result, and authorized decision;
- **Match** evidence requirements and decision rights to reversibility, blast radius, and consequences;
- **Inspect** a rejection path through its condition, executing check, independence basis, blocked transition, check owner, and waiver authority;
- **Explain** why architects must understand the loop even when tools execute routine checks.
:::

## Feedback Cost and Availability

@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces used feedback regimes to design
environments, and @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization used them to
choose method roles. This chapter uses the same economics to decide what
evidence the loop can obtain before a decision.

The first practical limit is feedback cost and availability. Feedback is not free, uniform, or
automatically useful. An architecture loop may have thousands of cheap proxy
evaluations, hundreds of simulations, tens of synthesis or physical-design
runs, a few emulation opportunities, and almost no chances to learn from
silicon or deployment mistakes. It may also have scarce human review time,
limited tool licenses, long queue delays, confidential workloads, and
organizational deadlines. @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales already measured
this limit. Verification staffing on IC/ASIC projects sits at rough parity
with design staffing, and projects spend roughly half their schedule in
verification, before any AI-generated candidate stream arrives
(@fig-ch07-verification-economics). Available review capacity depends on people as well as
compute. These limits affect which methods are
appropriate and which decision rights may be delegated.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch07-verification-economics
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Verification staffing and schedule share.** Across the Wilson Research Group / Siemens EDA functional-verification study waves, mean peak verification staffing on IC/ASIC projects rose from roughly 0.6 verification engineer per design engineer in 2007 to rough parity from 2012 onward, with processor projects reaching as much as 5:1, while projects spent roughly half their schedule in verification [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. The left panel shows the staffing ratio; the head-count growth behind it appears in @fig-verification-demand-scissors. The 2022 ratio derives from unlabeled study charts and is approximate; the 2022 time share is the study's reported 50-60 percent median.
#| out-width: "86%"
#| fig-alt: "Two-panel plot of Wilson Research Group functional-verification study data. Left panel: verification-to-design engineer ratio on IC/ASIC projects rising from about 0.6 in 2007 to about 1.0 to 1.1 from 2012 through 2022, with a note that processor projects reach up to five to one. Right panel: share of project time spent in verification staying near 53 to 57 percent from 2014 through 2020, with a 50 to 60 percent median band for 2022."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter7-wilson-verification-economics.csv
# Wilson Research Group / Siemens EDA functional-verification studies (IC/ASIC),
# via Foster's published study summaries [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy].
# Left panel shows the verification-to-design staffing RATIO in levels,
# derived from the study's printed mean-peak staffing labels (2022 from
# chart-read bars constrained by the stated +50% design / +146% verification
# growth since 2007). The underlying head-count pairs are receipted with the
# ch02 scissors figure (chapter2-wilson-verification-staffing.csv); this
# chapter reads only their ratio. Time-share values 2014-2020 are study-figure
# annotations; 2022 is a stated 50-60% median.
ratio_series = [
    # (year, verification engineers per design engineer, approximate?)
    (2007, 0.615, False),
    (2012, 0.988, False),
    (2016, 1.105, False),
    (2020, 1.107, False),
    (2022, 1.009, True),
]
time_share = [(2014, 57), (2016, 54), (2018, 53), (2020, 56)]
time_2022_band = (50, 60)  # stated median range, 2022 wave

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(4.65, 1.95))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.105, right=0.985, top=0.86, bottom=0.185, wspace=0.42)

years = [s[0] for s in ratio_series]
ratios = [s[1] for s in ratio_series]
ax1.axhline(1.0, color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.8, zorder=0)
ax1.plot(years, ratios, color=COLORS["evidence"], linewidth=1.4, zorder=2)
solid = [(y, r) for y, r, approx_flag in ratio_series if not approx_flag]
approx = [(y, r) for y, r, approx_flag in ratio_series if approx_flag]
ax1.scatter([p[0] for p in solid], [p[1] for p in solid], s=16,
            color=COLORS["evidence"], zorder=3)
ax1.scatter([p[0] for p in approx], [p[1] for p in approx], s=18,
            facecolor="white", edgecolor=COLORS["evidence"], linewidth=1.0,
            zorder=3)
ax1.annotate("parity", (2007.4, 1.0), textcoords="offset points",
             xytext=(0, 3), fontsize=5.5, color=COLORS["muted"])
ax1.annotate("processor projects:\nup to 5:1", (2013.2, 0.68), fontsize=5.5,
             color=COLORS["evidence_ink"])
ax1.set_ylim(0.4, 1.35)
ax1.set_ylabel("verification / design\nengineers (mean peak)", fontsize=6.4)
ax1.set_xticks([2007, 2012, 2016, 2022])
ax1.set_title("Staffing ratio, IC/ASIC projects", fontsize=6.6, pad=4)

ax2.bar([t[0] for t in time_share], [t[1] for t in time_share], width=1.15,
        color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=2)
lo, hi = time_2022_band
ax2.bar([2022], [hi - lo], bottom=lo, width=1.15, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"],
        edgecolor=COLORS["blue"], linewidth=0.8, zorder=2)
ax2.annotate("50-60%\n(median)", (2022, hi), textcoords="offset points",
             xytext=(0, 3), ha="center", fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["workload_ink"])
ax2.set_ylim(0, 78)
ax2.set_ylabel("project time in\nverification (%)", fontsize=6.4)
ax2.set_xticks([2014, 2018, 2022])
ax2.set_title("Schedule share in verification", fontsize=6.6, pad=4)

for ax in (ax1, ax2):
    ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
    ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55)
    for spine in ["top", "right"]:
        ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
    ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
```

Because detailed tool runs and expert review are scarce, the
supporting cost record states which
evaluations, measurements, tool runs, human reviews, and deployment signals are
available, what they cost, how long they take, how reversible they are, and
which decisions they can support. The record shows what kind of learning
and checking is possible. A Bayesian optimizer[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimizer-budget], reinforcement-learning policy[^fn-rl-policy],
surrogate model[^fn-ml-surrogate-proxy-feedback], critic[^fn-rl-critic], or tool-using agent should behave differently when a
feedback source takes milliseconds versus days, when a failed action is
reversible versus costly, and when the signal is a rough proxy versus a
signoff report (@tbl-feedback-budget).

[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimizer-budget]: The feedback-budget issue is how aggressively the optimizer may sample. A cheap proxy budget supports broad search; a costly synthesis budget demands selective acquisition and a fidelity record for each evaluation.

[^fn-rl-policy]: In reinforcement learning, a policy is a function that maps an agent's observed state to a chosen action. Treated here as a method inside the loop, its output advances only through the evidence its actions leave and the checks that can reject them.

[^fn-ml-surrogate-proxy-feedback]: For this surrogate use, evidence scope is the issue. A surrogate's ranking can guide exploration, but it should remain proxy feedback until an independent, decision-relevant check can reject a proxy win.

[^fn-rl-critic]: In this book, a critic is the critique method role of @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization, a component that challenges assumptions, evidence, or claims inside the loop; reinforcement learning uses the same word more narrowly for a learned estimator of expected future returns. Here a critic supplies a challenge. It becomes a rejection check only when a declared condition can block the candidate independently of the generating method.

| **Budget item** | **What to record** | **Why it matters** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Latency and cost** | Runtime, queue time, dollar cost, tool hours, license limits, model calls, GPU-hours, energy or carbon exposure, and human review time. | Determines whether the loop should search broadly, sample carefully, or mostly critique. |
| **Signal quality** | Fidelity level, metric definition, noise, determinism, coverage, and uncertainty. | Separates raw feedback from decision-grade evidence. |
| **Sample budget** | Number of possible runs at each fidelity, including failed runs and invalid candidates. | Forces sample-efficient methods and preserves failed runs and rejected alternatives. |
| **Reversibility** | Whether the action can be undone cheaply, re-run, patched, or rolled back. | Connects delegated execution to consequence. Reversible actions can tolerate a different evidence burden than difficult-to-reverse decisions. |
| **Decision impact** | Claim level supported, condition triggered, transition blocked, and decision owner. | Ties each feedback source to the decision it can change. |
| **Scarce attention** | Expert review, debugging effort, validation bandwidth, security review, and integration time. | Prevents the loop from outsourcing cost to people whose time is the real bottleneck. |

: **Feedback budgets state the cost of learning:** The supporting record states what feedback is available, what it costs, what evidence it can support, and when scarce expert attention or irreversible action should limit automation. {#tbl-feedback-budget tbl-colwidths="[22,34,32]"}

Cost records help reviewers compare studies that use feedback differently. A
method that finds a good point after 10,000 cheap proxy evaluations provides
different evidence from a method that selects three candidates for expensive
synthesis. A study that records failures, timeouts, warnings, rejected
candidates, and review notes provides more information than one that records
only the winning score.

The following notation is a reminder to compare the expected decision value of
an evaluation with its cost. It is not intended as a universal numerical score.

$$
B = \sum_i n_i \cdot c_i,
\qquad
V_i \approx \frac{\Delta \mathrm{Conf}(d \mid e_i)}{c_i}.
$$

Here, $n_i$ is the number of evaluations of feedback type $i$, $c_i$ is
the cost of one such evaluation, $B$ is the total feedback budget, $e_i$
is the supporting evidence produced by that evaluation, $\Delta \mathrm{Conf}(d
\mid e_i)$ is the change in confidence for the decision $d$, and $V_i$ is the
resulting decision value of one such evaluation. It is the per-feedback-type
form of the sample-value rule from
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization; ordinal confidence changes or
decision-relevance labels are often more accurate than unjustified numeric
confidence. As one evaluation's cost rises from milliseconds for an analytic proxy to months for silicon or deployment, the
affordable sample count collapses toward one (@fig-ch07-feedback-funnel).

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch07-feedback-funnel
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **The feedback funnel sets the budget the notation formalizes:** As the cost of one evaluation rises from milliseconds for an analytic proxy to months for silicon or deployment evidence, the affordable sample count $n_i$ under a fixed budget $B = \sum_i n_i \cdot c_i$ collapses from thousands toward one. Ranges are illustrative orders of magnitude from this chapter's funnel; the cycle-level source is anchored to the wall-clock arithmetic of @sec-data-representations-world-models (one second of a 1 GHz target at 100 kcycle/s to 10 Mcycle/s of simulator throughput).
#| out-width: "86%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-log plot with cost per evaluation in seconds on the horizontal axis spanning milliseconds to months, and evaluations per study on the vertical axis spanning one to one hundred thousand. Five labeled boxes descend from analytic proxy at thousands to a hundred thousand cheap evaluations, through cycle-level simulation in the hundreds, synthesis and physical design in the tens, emulation at a few, down to silicon or deployment at about one."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Illustrative orders of magnitude from this chapter's funnel sentence
# ("thousands of cheap proxy evaluations, hundreds of simulations, tens of
# synthesis or physical-design runs, a few emulation opportunities, and almost
# no chances to learn from silicon"). The cycle-level cost span replicates
# ch04's wall-clock computation T_wall = f_target * T_workload / R_sim for a
# 1 GHz target running a 1 s workload at the mid-range illustrative
# throughputs of its simulation-time table (100 kcycle/s and 10 Mcycle/s).
f_target, t_workload = 1e9, 1.0
sim_cost = (f_target * t_workload / 1e7, f_target * t_workload / 1e5)

feedback_sources = [
    # (label, cost range in seconds, evaluations range, color key)
    ("Analytic proxy", (1e-3, 1e0), (1e3, 1e5), "blue"),
    ("Cycle-level simulation", sim_cost, (1e2, 1e3), "purple"),
    ("Synthesis / physical design", (1e4, 3e5), (1e1, 1e2), "orange"),
    ("Emulation", (1e5, 1e6), (2, 8), "red"),
    ("Silicon / deployment", (3e6, 3e7), (1, 2), "magenta"),
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.3))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.115, right=0.97, top=0.95, bottom=0.19)
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_yscale("log")

label_offsets = {
    "Analytic proxy": (0, 8),
    "Cycle-level simulation": (0, 8),
    "Synthesis / physical design": (0, 8),
    "Emulation": (-6, 8),
    "Silicon / deployment": (0, 8),
}
for label, (c_lo, c_hi), (n_lo, n_hi), key in feedback_sources:
    ax.fill_betweenx([n_lo, n_hi], c_lo, c_hi, facecolor=COLORS[key],
                     alpha=0.22, edgecolor=COLORS[key], linewidth=0.9, zorder=2)
    cx = (c_lo * c_hi) ** 0.5
    dx, dy = label_offsets[label]
    ax.annotate(label, (cx, n_hi), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(dx, dy),
                ha="center", fontsize=5.7, fontweight="bold",
                color=COLORS.get(key + "_ink", COLORS[key]))

ax.set_xlim(3e-4, 3e8)
ax.set_ylim(0.5, 4e5)
ax.set_xticks([1e-3, 1e0, 60, 3600, 86400, 2.6e6])
ax.set_xticklabels(["1 ms", "1 s", "1 min", "1 h", "1 day", "1 month"])
ax.set_xlabel("cost per evaluation (log scale)", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("evaluations per study (log scale)", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(True, which="major", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.5)
for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
```

Architects should compare confidence gains with computational and human costs, much as the quantitative tradition compares performance with area and power. For example, spending 100 CPU-hours ($c_i$) on a cycle-level simulation to gain a modest reduction in power, performance, and area (PPA) uncertainty ($\Delta \mathrm{Conf}$) might be less valuable than spending 1 hour of expert review to catch a fundamental security flaw. Before requesting another simulation, synthesis run, review, or deployment experiment, the study owner should ask whether the result could change the decision enough to justify its cost. @sec-running-the-loop
makes the question concrete by fixing equal four-run SCALE-Sim budgets for the
model and conventional arms. Energy, area, compiler, RTL, and physical-design
questions are deferred because answering them would require a new evidence plan.
The cost record should identify the candidate, requested evaluation, expected
result, possible failure, and decision it would affect.

## Evidence Must Fit the Consequence {#sec-evidence-by-consequence}

Feedback becomes evidence only when it is tied to fidelity, provenance,
uncertainty, and a decision. A simulator result is feedback. It becomes
evidence when the workload, simulator version, configuration, random seed,
assumptions, metric definition, failure status, and acceptance criterion are
recorded. A synthesis report is feedback. It becomes evidence when the
technology assumptions, constraints, tool versions, warnings, and comparison
baseline are recorded.

Evidence types cannot be ranked on a universal scale of fidelity. A cycle
estimate, synthesis report, formal proof, post-route result, and deployment
trace answer different questions under different assumptions. For each
evidence record, record the dimensions in @tbl-evidence-dimensions instead of
treating tool stages as a single confidence score.

| **Dimension** | **Question the record must answer** |
| --- | --- |
| **Producer and status** | Who or what produced the result, and is it measured, computed, digitized, illustrative, or author judgment? |
| **Source and fidelity** | Which simulator, tool stage, experiment, review, or deployment path produced the observation, at what cost? |
| **Scope** | Which workload, design state, technology assumptions, objective, and comparison does the observation cover? |
| **Uncertainty and limits** | What variability, calibration error, missing behavior, or known blind spot bounds the result? |
| **Integrity and provenance** | Which versions, inputs, outputs, commands, hashes, and access controls associate the record with the run? |
| **Independence** | Which actors, models, data, objectives, and tool paths are shared with the producer, and which are independent? |

: **Dimensions of an evidence record.** Adequacy depends on the claim
and the next decision. Expensive evidence can still be out of scope, and cheap
evidence can be decisive for a narrow rejection criterion. {#tbl-evidence-dimensions tbl-colwidths="[24,66]"}

This way of connecting claims to evidence is not new to engineering.
Safety-critical fields formalize it as an *assurance case*, a structured
argument linking a top-level claim to sub-claims, assumptions, supporting
evidence, and defeaters. Goal Structuring Notation[^fn-safety-gsn] makes those
links traceable. Architecture 2.0 uses a lighter claim-to-decision
chain, but it borrows the same structure. Together, the supporting evidence and
review records show which observations support the claim, which failed checks
or rejected alternatives weaken it, and which waivers or decision rights
allowed it to continue. Assurance cases make common failures easier to find,
including unstated assumptions, evidence outside the claimed scope, and
conclusions that the evidence does not support.

[^fn-safety-gsn]: Goal Structuring Notation (GSN) is a graphical argumentation notation used in safety-critical engineering to document the elements of an assurance case, making each inference and its supporting evidence traceable [@Kelly2004GSN]. The claim-to-decision chain borrows this structure so that a design claim is reported with its support, assumptions, counterevidence, and decision rather than as a bare score.

When a claim needs several feedback sources, an Architecture 2.0 study should
record a scoped observation, supporting or contrary evidence, and result for
each one. Each rejection check should target a specific failure. Reviewers
should consider all supporting and contradictory results together, then leave
the final decision to the person assigned that responsibility. No model output,
tool stage, or score can authorize the proposed transition on its own.

Ordering tools by cost is not a ranking from false to true. A detailed
physical-design result can still answer the wrong architecture question. A
deployment signal can still be confounded by a software change. A benchmark
can still be too narrow. Staging is useful because each source can challenge a
different assumption. Evidence from a later stage does not automatically
support a broader decision.

The executed study in @sec-running-the-loop makes the boundary concrete.
SCALE-Sim supplies computed cycle and utilization evidence under a frozen
mapping model. That evidence can compare the legal array geometries in the
study. It cannot establish energy, area, compiler behavior, RTL correctness, or
physical feasibility. The baseline-retention recommendation fits the evidence;
an implementation decision would not.

This framing is consistent with the quantitative tradition in computer
architecture, where measurement, abstraction, and careful comparison are
central [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Architecture 2.0
adds one requirement. People responsible for reviewing or approving a
recommendation must be able to inspect the evidence and its limitations, and
automated components must expose the inputs and results needed for that review.

### Consequence Sets the Evidence Burden {#sec-consequence-and-reversibility}

The evidence required for a candidate depends on the action being considered.
A simulator configuration, RTL change, chiplet boundary, package interface,
signoff waiver, and fleet rollout have different rollback costs, affected
interfaces, and consequences. The burden of proof depends on who bears those
consequences when the study is wrong, regardless of whether AI is involved.

@tbl-evidence-by-consequence gives representative consequence classes. They are
not maturity levels. The same candidate can move from one row to another as the
proposed action changes, and each move requires a fresh review of the evidence
and decision rights. An escalation threshold states when the current evidence
can no longer support the proposed transition.

A proxy result may justify another experiment, but not an interface change,
signoff waiver, or power claim. As an action becomes harder to reverse or
affects more people, the study needs stronger evidence and approval from the
appropriate owner. The study should state in advance which events require that
additional review. Examples include uncertainty outside the calibrated range,
a benchmark coverage gap, a failed tool check, a security boundary, a high
rollback cost, or an action that affects another team or product.

Architects do not need to execute every routine check, but they must understand
the chain. They set the claim scope, decide what execution may be delegated,
judge evidence sufficiency, and identify who may grant waivers and who owns the
system consequences. An organization may distribute those rights across design,
verification, security, product, or release roles. The card must record that
distribution rather than treating every check as a singular human decision or
as an autonomous tool choice.

| **Proposed transition** | **Consequence cues** | **Evidence and review requirements** | **Rights that must be stated** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Continue local exploration | Candidate can be discarded; no shared interface or external obligation changes. | Basic validity, provenance, scope, and a comparison sufficient to decide what to test next. | Propose and execute scope; automatic reject conditions; study owner. |
| Advance a bounded experiment | Scarce tool time, shared data, or a deeper test is consumed, but rollback remains local. | Versioned workload, matched baseline, uncertainty, failed alternatives, and criteria for stopping or escalating. | Execute budget, rejection authority, recommender, and evidence owner. |
| Change an implementation or interface | RTL, generator, compiler, runtime, memory, chiplet, security, or team contract changes. | Regression and integration evidence, compatibility checks, cross-tool challenge, and residual-risk review. | Rejector, waiver holder, recommender, affected-interface owners, and commit right. |
| Authorize signoff or consequential deployment | Rollback is difficult, customer- or silicon-facing effects are possible, or a formal obligation is accepted. | Independent evidence appropriate to the claim, fully specified rejection checks, audit trail, recorded waivers, and reopening conditions. | Separate reject and waiver rights where needed, plus a named commit-right holder. |

: **Evidence burden follows consequence:** Reversibility, blast radius, affected
interfaces, and check independence determine what must be shown and who may
advance the transition. {#tbl-evidence-by-consequence tbl-colwidths="[20,23,30,19]"}

A study may automate routine work while reserving the rights to reject, waive,
recommend, or commit for different people. Some useful systems make no final
design choices. They narrow the design space, identify contradictions, preserve
evidence, and help the responsible people decide.

::: {#pri-escalate-commitment .callout-design-principle title="Require more evidence as consequences grow"}
A result that justifies another experiment may not justify an interface change
or signoff decision. As rollback becomes harder, more systems or people are
affected, or independent checks become more important, require stronger
evidence and name the role authorized to proceed.
:::

Stronger evidence matters only if a failed check can block an unsupported
transition.

## Rejection Authority

@sec-architecture-20-ontology defined rejection authority as the decision right
to block a candidate or claim. Exercising that right requires an inspectable
rejection path. The executing check may be a parser, simulator, formal tool,
regression test, synthesis flow, cross-tool comparison, signoff rule,
deployment signal, security policy, benchmark rule, or expert review. Its
rejection condition may detect a proxy mismatch, protocol violation, physical
limit, or policy boundary.

Early automated checks can reduce the number of candidates that require
expensive review. Their benefit depends on how many cases they settle
accurately and how much human or tool time each check consumes. Routing, audit
cost, reference checks, and concurrency also affect review capacity; a
quantitative model appears in @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack. Search methods
can reduce the load as well if they require fewer evaluations to reach a given
quality target.

When a check rejects a candidate, the record should identify the failed
condition, the check that found it, the action it blocked, the check owner, and
any waiver process. @tbl-rejection-review-properties lists the information a
reviewer needs.

| **Review property** | **What the record must state** |
| --- | --- |
| **Rejection condition** | The exact observation or missing requirement that causes failure. |
| **Executing check** | The tool, test, policy, review process, or deployment signal that evaluates the condition. |
| **Independence basis** | What the check does not share with the generator, such as model family, training data, objective, tool path, workload, or evidence source. |
| **Transition it can block** | The state change or decision that cannot proceed after failure. |
| **Check owner** | The role responsible for the check's operation, maintenance, and interpretation. |
| **Waiver authority** | The distinct role or process allowed to override the failure, with rationale, scope, expiration, and reopening conditions. |

: **Information required to review a rejection.** Naming a check is
insufficient unless the record also states the condition, independence basis,
blocked action, owner, and waiver authority. {#tbl-rejection-review-properties tbl-colwidths="[25,65]"}

An invalid constraint, timing miss, benchmark violation, or expert objection can
identify a design-space boundary. A simulator crash or failed build is recorded
first as an environment failure and becomes a candidate rejection only when a
declared validity or constraint rule makes the candidate responsible for that
outcome.

Before a transition proceeds, its rejection check should answer three questions:

1. **Is the candidate legal?** It violates no declared structural, ISA,
   software, interface, security, or policy contract.
2. **Does the evidence meet the named criteria?** The required records exist,
   refer to this claim and comparison, cover the stated scope, and survive the
   declared challenge checks.
3. **Has the commit-right holder accepted the residual risk?** The
   unresolved uncertainty, consequences, waiver state, and reopening conditions
   are available to whoever holds the commit right.

These conditions are sets of requirements, not scalar scores. A failed item
causes rejection, revision, or escalation. Only the assigned waiver holder may
override a failed check. The waiver rationale, scope, expiration, and reopening
conditions become part of the review record.

The executed study in @sec-running-the-loop shows the distinction. Its legal
geometry and deadline checks can reject a candidate inside the SCALE-Sim study.
Its evidence can support a narrow recommendation about the tested workload and
cycle objective. It cannot support an energy, area, RTL, or physical-design
transition because those records do not exist. The mechanism probe also fails,
so the explanation is rejected even though every evaluated geometry is legal.

For a claim to be comparable across teams, its rejection criteria cannot remain
private judgment calls. The teams must agree on the workload, metric, baseline,
tool versions, budgets, and reference checks. Otherwise, a result may satisfy
one team's rules without being comparable with another team's result.

A failed check must change the work. The team might discard the candidate,
record missing state, add an environment validity test, restrict the method's
actions, narrow workload coverage, weaken the claim, or escalate the decision.
Without such a response, a failed check is only a log message.

This rejection authority also protects against polished but unsupported outputs.
Tool-using agents can generate convincing summaries, plots, design reports,
and review notes. Those artifacts are useful only when a failed check can block
their use. In architecture, a polished explanation cannot overrule a failed
timing check, an invalid workload, a missing baseline, or a security boundary.
A deployed version of this independence appears at fleet scale in
@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack. During an instruction-set migration, build,
test, sanitizers, and a production monitor evict regressions automatically,
regardless of how confident the automated repair tool was.

The independence requirement grows sharper as verification itself becomes
AI-assisted. Machine-learning-assisted verification tools, such as Cadence
Verisium for triaging failures, ranking likely root causes, and directing
coverage, can be valuable [@Cadence2022Verisium]. But they move part of the
verification work into a learned system. Reviewers must therefore ask whether
the check is independent of the system that produced the design. AI-assisted
verification can share failure modes with generation, particularly when the
two systems use the same model family, training data, objective, or evidence.
In that case, agreement does not provide an independent check.

The same rule applies when rejection is split across components. A generator,
critic, verifier, and summarizer may be separate components in an implementation,
but they do not become independent merely by having different prompts or names.
Independence depends on what they share, such as training data, model family, objective,
tool path, evidence source, or human instruction. Software fault tolerance
learned this empirically. Knight and Leveson's multiversion-programming
experiment found that independently developed program versions fail on
correlated inputs far more often than an independence assumption predicts
[@KnightLeveson1986Multiversion]. A loop that asks several
correlated components to agree has created consensus, not rejection authority,
unless a declared failure can block the candidate regardless of the generator's
explanation.

## Proxy Mismatch, Metric Gaming, and Calibration {#sec-proxy-mismatch-metric-gaming-and-calibration}

Proxy mismatch, defined in @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces, is a
recurring failure that rejection checks must catch. A proxy win may justify
further evaluation, but it supports a consequential decision only after an
independent, decision-relevant check provides evidence that the optimized metric
supports the intended architecture claim.

The appropriate verification response depends on the source of the mismatch. A
poorly calibrated proxy or one applied outside its validated region may need
better fitting or broader validation. A search method may instead exploit an
objective that omits an important cost, as in the reward hacking described in
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces. For example, it might improve
estimated wire delay by moving a compute block while ignoring the resulting
thermal hotspot. An independent thermal check is then necessary because
improving the proxy alone may not prevent the search from finding another
version of the same flaw.

AI did not create this problem. Benchmarks, simulators, cost models,
and design rules have always been approximations. What changes in Architecture
2.0 is the speed and persistence with which a method can exploit the
approximation. A human may notice that a score is improving for the wrong
reason, while a search method can continue exploiting it. A compound system may
even produce a persuasive narrative for a proxy win unless the study requires
calibration and counterevidence.

The AlphaChip case tests this distinction at placement scale. Here it shows why
a proxy score and a post-route evaluation support different claims rather than
resolving the broader public dispute.

::: {.callout-war-story title="A proxy under dispute"}
**The claim.** AlphaChip, a reinforcement-learning method for macro placement, optimized a
fast proxy for the metrics used to judge a completed
placement. The work became the subject of a broader dispute over whether that
proxy rewarded the right designs.

**The gap.** A 2023 reassessment reported weak correlation between the proxy and post-route
chip-quality metrics, including little useful
correlation with timing [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. A placement could score
well under the proxy without a corresponding improvement after a full
physical-design flow.

**The lesson.** The dispute also concerned pretraining, compute, implementation, and test
cases. Its narrower lesson here is that an improvement
under a proxy remains preliminary until a decision-relevant evaluation confirms
it. A proxy result can justify further testing, but not a final design decision.
:::

Calibration should specify where a proxy may rank candidates and when a
stronger evaluation is required. Architecture predictors have long been
validated against held-out data[^fn-ml-held-out-data], more
decision-relevant measurements, or carefully designed experiments
[@LeeBrooks2006RegressionModeling; @IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. The same rule
applies to AI-assisted studies. If a study claims that a candidate satisfies a
power envelope, its evidence should state how the estimate was calibrated,
which workload region it covers, what uncertainty remains, and what result
would overturn the claim.

[^fn-ml-held-out-data]: Held-out data is a subset of a dataset reserved during training and used only to test whether a model generalizes to unseen inputs. A prediction validated against held-out cases provides stronger evidence than one fitted and reported on the same data.

The practical question is whether the candidate remains inside the region for
which the proxy was validated.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Calibration Check"}
Has the study established when a generator's proxy leaves calibrated support?
A proxy win supports a claim only when the candidate falls within that region.
If it falls outside, the study must seek a decision-relevant or independent
check rather than treat the prediction as evidence.
:::

Calibration does not preserve comparability by itself. Benchmark versions and
run rules must also be recorded because changes to them
can alter a result. As @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales and
@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack show, shared benchmarks need published rules,
versions, and submission practices if results are to remain comparable. When a
benchmark changes, affected claims should be reevaluated rather than compared
as though the setup were unchanged.

## Security, IP, and Confidentiality Boundaries

Calibration, provenance, and benchmark rules make a claim assessable, but a
consequential decision still requires review by an authorized person. Evidence
may remain confidential, but the study should state who can inspect it and what
that review can establish. RTL, design specifications, process assumptions, timing
constraints, floorplans, tool logs, customer workloads, proprietary traces,
compiler settings, design reviews, and deployment telemetry can all reveal
valuable or restricted information.

The study should identify which data must remain private, which tools may
access it, what can be shared or redacted, and who can inspect the complete
record. For generated designs, the same record should cover license provenance,
ownership, export or release classification, and disclosure authority. These
technical records support the review; they do not replace appropriate legal or
organizational review.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Policy Boundary Check"}
Can the evidence be reviewed without violating security, IP, license,
export-control, or disclosure restrictions? The card should name which AI tools
can access each data class, how an authorized reviewer can verify private
evidence, and who may approve redaction, compliance review, waivers, and
release.
:::

This requirement also defines the bounds of microarchitectural security. A loop
that optimizes IPC without an isolation constraint may propose structures that
improve measured performance by sharing predictors, translation lookaside
buffers (TLBs), caches, or buffers across trust boundaries while the proxy
leaves side-channel risk unpriced. An early isolation screen such as
@lst-side-channel can reject an RTL candidate that shares speculative state
across those boundaries. It is necessary but not sufficient, since it misses
contention, transient-execution, and simultaneous multithreading (SMT) channels.
A decision-relevant security review may still require formal non-interference (a guarantee that a component's
secret state cannot affect what an untrusted observer sees) [@GoguenMeseguer1982] or side-channel verification where the proposed transition requires it. A complete security
screen also covers memory-integrity and disturbance faults (where repeated
access to one memory location corrupts a neighboring one), not only
information-flow side channels. A design that trims memory-integrity margins to
meet a power budget can make read disturbance a rejection criterion in its own right.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{20\baselineskip}
```

::: {.callout-field-note title="What Spectre's Missing Check Exposed"}
For decades, improving instructions per cycle has been a powerful
microarchitectural optimization pressure, pursued through increasingly
aggressive speculation and reuse of predictors, caches, and buffers across
execution contexts. Ordinary functional tests could pass because speculation
computed the correct architectural results. In 2018 that same optimization
pressure was shown to have built a class of side channels that leak across
privilege boundaries [@KocherEtAl2019Spectre]. Ordinary functional checks did
not catch it because the objective the loop could see, IPC, and the leakage it
could not, were different quantities.

Functional correctness does not establish security. If non-interference and
timing-channel evidence sit outside the study, performance wins can accumulate
risk that ordinary correctness tests never see.
:::

The following illustrative check captures only the early structural part of
that security requirement.

```{.python #lst-side-channel lst-cap="**Early security check:** An illustrative Python snippet rejecting RTL candidates that share state across trust boundaries."}
def check_side_channel_interference(rtl_candidate, isolation_policy):
    """
    Early security check. Reject RTL that shares speculative structures
    across defined security domains before physical synthesis.
    """
    shared_structures = find_shared_state(
        rtl_candidate, type=["BPU", "Cache", "TLB"]
    )
    for struct in shared_structures:
        if crosses_domain(struct, isolation_policy):
            return Reject(
                reason=(f"Security violation: {struct.name} shares "
                        "state across trust boundaries."),
                action="Partition the structure or flush on context switch."
            )
    return EscalateToSecurityVerification()
```

Codified as the screen in @lst-side-channel, this check catches the most obvious performance-for-isolation trades early. Passing the check permits the candidate to advance to additional security verification; it does not authorize the design itself. A surviving candidate must still undergo formal non-interference or side-channel verification (for example, information-flow tracking) before a commit decision.

Shared research infrastructure must distinguish public artifacts from private
state. Public benchmarks, datasets, papers, and tool environments support
comparison, while private workloads, proprietary RTL, product traces, and
process-specific assumptions often cannot be released. The design-loop card
should state the restricted data class, which tools may access it, which
conclusions a public proxy cannot support, what authorized reviewers can
inspect, what cannot be disclosed, and who may approve an exception.
Reproducibility may then mean authorized audit rather than public release.

## Evidence Disputes and Decision Review {#sec-evidence-disputes-and-the-trust-checklist}

Even after accuracy and policy boundaries are stated, reviewers may disagree
about the baseline, workload, assumptions, tool setup, missing failures, or
decision criteria. Private evidence may further limit what can be released.
These disagreements require access to the complete review record and a
designated person or process to decide whether the claim is accepted, rejected,
or narrowed.

@fig-evidence-dispute-anatomy shows the information needed to evaluate a
contested claim. Model confidence does not settle the dispute. Reviewers inspect
the evidence, assumptions, baseline, contrary results, and failed checks. A
named role then applies the declared rules and decides whether to accept,
reject, or escalate the claim.

![**Evidence and decision authority are reviewed separately.** A contested architecture claim is reported with its supporting evidence, fidelity, scope, provenance, baseline, contrary evidence, and failed checks. A named reviewer or process decides whether to accept, reject, or escalate it under the declared rules.](images/F9b-evidence-dispute-anatomy){#fig-evidence-dispute-anatomy width="100%" fig-alt="Flow diagram separating a contested architecture claim's evidence packet from the named review role authorized to accept, reject, or escalate it."}

The AlphaChip case in the war story can now be read through this review
structure. The questions it raises about baselines, released code, and
evaluation conditions belong in the review record
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. A
model-confidence score cannot settle them. End-to-end benchmarks such as
ChiPBench address part of the problem by evaluating final power, performance,
and area rather than only an intermediate proxy
[@WangEtAl2025ChiPBench]. A placement study also needs the represented state,
legal actions, workload, baseline, toolchain versions, constraints, seeds,
rejected alternatives, and model weights needed to interpret or reproduce its
result. @sec-alphachip-public-record-audit applies these questions to the public
record and identifies which details can and cannot be aligned.

@tbl-trust-checklist summarizes the questions a reviewer should ask. It can be
used for papers, internal tools, student projects, and studies that propose a
more consequential action.

| **Question** | **What a credible answer contains** | **Warning sign** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **What output is under review?** | Candidate, ranking, report, code/RTL change, or recommended transition, with generator and method role named. | The result is described only as an AI improvement. |
| **What is the claim?** | A bounded architecture task, comparator, objective, workload, scope, non-claims, and proposed transition. | Vague claims of automation or improvement. |
| **What loop state and action contract are exposed?** | State fields, legal actions, tool or environment APIs, invalid transitions, and replay packages. | The loop can act through hidden state or unlogged tool semantics. |
| **What evidence supports it?** | Claim-relevant metrics, tool outputs, logs, and reviews with their available evaluation resources. | Only the winning score is shown. |
| **What weakens or blocks it?** | Counterevidence, failed runs, rejected alternatives, coverage gaps, warnings, and unresolved disagreements. | Contrary results or failed checks are omitted. |
| **What is the fidelity?** | The record states proxy, simulation, synthesis, emulation, signoff, deployment, or silicon level. | Treating all measurements as equivalent. |
| **What is the provenance?** | Workload and exact tool versions, tech nodes, configs, seeds, run-to-run variance or other nondeterminism, constraints, assumptions, and baselines needed for the declared audit or replay claim. | Hidden scripts, unstated defaults, or unrecorded nondeterminism. |
| **What is the security, IP, license, export-control, or disclosure boundary?** | Data classes, access rules, redaction limits, license or disclosure class, release or export-control review record, public proxy limits, authorized reviewer role, and an audit path. | Private or restricted evidence is treated as invisible or unverifiable. |
| **What can reject it?** | The rejection condition, executing check, independence basis, transition blocked, check owner, and waiver authority. | A check is named without the right or independence needed to block anything. |
| **Who holds the commit right?** | A named role or process with accepted consequences, waiver state, residual risk, and reopening conditions. | The process silently treats output as a decision. |

: **Questions for reviewing a design claim.** A credible report includes its supporting and contrary evidence, fidelity, provenance, policy limits, rejection and waiver process, decision rights, and owners before the proposed action proceeds. {#tbl-trust-checklist tbl-colwidths="[24,38,26]"}

The checklist translates the design-loop card and its supporting files into a
review sequence. A paper, tool, or project does not need to solve a full
subsystem problem to be valuable. It should state its contribution, the
evidence it produces, its limitations, the checks that can reject its result,
and who may authorize the proposed action.

## Open Research Questions

Further work is needed on evidence formats, tests for proxy exploitation,
independent verification, and review of confidential results.

1. **How should supporting evidence records limit a decision?**
The evidence-by-consequence view assumes that each record remains attached to
the claim it supports. A fundamental systems challenge is
to design a record format and checker that associate proxy observations, simulation
runs, synthesis results, waivers, rejection events,
redactions, and reopening conditions into a unified audit object. Its value
would be measured by tamper detection, replayability, and whether the checker
prevents a transition whose evidence or decision rights are incomplete.
@sec-evidence-by-consequence defines the boundary this checker must enforce.

2. **What benchmarks detect proxy exploitation before authorization?**
Proxy mismatch, metric gaming, and calibration failures become more severe when
generators learn to exploit cheap architectural models. A thesis-level
contribution would build adversarial proxy benchmarks that include misleading
wins, support-boundary violations, and candidates that need stronger evidence.
The evaluation should report how often a loop escalates before accepting a
misleading candidate, plus the false-reject and evidence-cost tradeoffs of that
escalation. The motivation comes directly from @sec-proxy-mismatch-metric-gaming-and-calibration.

3. **How can rejection checks be tested for independence?**
A rejection check is useful only if it can expose failures missed by the
method, proxy, dataset, or objective that produced the candidate. The research
artifact is an independence test for rejection checks, using withheld
workloads, independently configured tools, human review protocols, or
orthogonal checks. The test should measure which generating-method failures the
check catches and whether the generating method can optimize around it.

4. **How can confidential evidence remain auditable?**
Architecture evidence often depends on proprietary workloads, physical design
flows, licensed reports, PDK (Process Design Kit)-bound evidence, internal baselines, or deployment
data that cannot be shared directly.
The contribution is an audit process that reports fidelity, provenance,
rejection, and authorization boundaries while redacting protected design state. It
should state what a reviewer can verify, what remains private, who is authorized
to inspect the redacted evidence, and what kinds of claims are too dependent on
confidential or restricted evidence to support a public decision.

## Conclusion

Before acting on a claim, reviewers should confirm its scope, supporting and
contrary evidence, failed checks, assumptions, and limitations. The record
should state what can reject the proposed action, whether that check is
independent, who is responsible for it, and who may grant a waiver. The final decision should
identify the responsible person and the consequences being accepted.

Architects must understand this chain even when tools execute routine checks.
They set the claim scope, determine what execution may be delegated, judge
evidence sufficiency, and identify who may grant waivers and who owns
cross-system consequences. Those rights may be distributed across several named
roles, but no model-confidence score or automated tool inherits them.

Evidence requirements should rise with consequence. Cheap feedback can screen
candidates, but actions that are hard to reverse or affect more people need
stronger and more independent evidence. A proxy improvement is not sufficient
unless the study states its uncertainty, what could overturn it, and who is
authorized to approve the proposed action.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Advance a claim only after reviewing the supporting and contrary evidence, failed checks, waivers, and the owner authorized to decide.
- **Reader test:** What would overturn the AI-assisted claim, what action would that check block, who is responsible for the consequence, and who may waive the failure?
- **Up next:** @sec-running-the-loop applies these review questions to a complete design study and inspects the resulting records.
:::
